title: Localization and delocalization for interacting 1D quasiperiodic particles.
speaker: Ilya Kachkovskiy
abstract: We consider a system of two interacting one-dimensional quasiperiodic particles as an operator on $\ell^2(\mathbb Z^2)$. The fact that particle frequencies are identical, implies a new effect compared to generic 2D potentials: the presence of large coupling localization depends on symmetries of the single-particle potential.

proof_stages.label: Two-particle quasiperiodic Hamiltonian and localization problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines a self-adjoint two-particle discrete Schrödinger operator on ℓ²(Z²) with separable quasiperiodic one-body potentials and an interaction term, and frames the goal as Anderson localization, i.e. pure point spectrum with exponentially decaying eigenfunctions.

proof_stages.label: Motivations and diagonal-frequency challenge
proof_stages.summary: The model is positioned as a quasiperiodic analogue of random many-body localization and of physics models predicting interaction-induced delocalization, while known two-dimensional quasiperiodic localization theorems are shown not to directly handle the zero-measure diagonal frequency set (ω,ω).

proof_stages.label: One-particle quasiperiodic spectral dichotomy and duality
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews the one-dimensional analytic quasiperiodic Schrödinger operator, where large coupling yields localization and small coupling yields absolutely continuous spectrum, with Fourier/Aubry-type duality explaining the contrast with random models.

proof_stages.label: Symmetry obstruction from level sets of the one-body potential
proof_stages.summary: The potential V is classified as symmetric or asymmetric according to translation-adjusted odd/half-period sign-change symmetries, because straight-line pieces in level sets V(θ1)+V(θ2)=E create the exceptional-energy obstruction and the initial-scale issue in the proof.

proof_stages.label: Low-complexity interaction potentials
proof_stages.summary: The interaction U is assumed to have size at most a fixed constant times λ and polynomially many translated local box patterns, a condition that in particular makes U take finitely many values u_j that later become the suspicious energies.

proof_stages.label: Large-coupling localization theorem
proof_stages.summary: The main localization theorem states that for sufficiently large λ and after removing a frequency set of measure less than ε, the operator localizes on the whole spectrum for asymmetric V and outside logarithmically thick neighborhoods of the finitely many values u_j for symmetric V.

proof_stages.label: Cosine diagonal-interaction band and delocalization theorem
proof_stages.summary: For the special cosine potential with diagonal Hubbard interaction Aδ_{n1=n2}, a large interaction separates an interaction band, and an effective one-dimensional Schur/Lyapunov-Schmidt model gives localization when the quasiperiodic term dominates but nontrivial absolutely continuous spectrum when the effective potential is suppressed.

proof_stages.label: Green-function multiscale proof of localization
proof_stages.summary: The proof of Theorem 1 is sketched by controlling finite-box Green functions via induction on scales, counting bad translated boxes along the diagonal irrational orbit, excluding bad frequencies, and using the strengthened geometric control of bad phase sets to turn Green-function decay into exponential decay of generalized eigenfunctions.

examples.name: finite-range relative-position interaction
examples.note: Interactions of the form U(n1,n2)=f(n1-n2) with finite support illustrate both the physics motivation and the low-complexity condition, since only polynomially many local patterns appear under translations.
examples.engagement_reason: the finite-support relative-position interaction is checked against the low-complexity condition, and its delta-diagonal special case is analyzed in the later theorem
